journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Nutrition, Health & Aging

subj-group.subject: Effects of Diet Education Plus Light Resistance Training on Coronary Heart Disease Risk Factors

title-group.article-title: The effects of diet education plus light resistance training on coronary heart disease risk factors in community-dwelling older adults

contrib-group.aff: 1Department of Nutrition and Food Sciences, University of Rhode Island, Ranger Hall, 02881, Kingston, Rhode Island, USA
2Department of Kinesiology, University of Rhode Island, Independence Square III, 02881, Kingston, Rhode Island, USA

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To examine the effects of diet education (DE) plus light resistance training (RT) on coronary heart disease risk (CHD) factors, specifically dietary quality, blood lipid and C-Reactive protein (CRP) concentrations in overweight and obese older adults in a community setting.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: Community outreach intervention with a quasi-experimental design.

sec.title: Participants and Setting
sec.p: A total of 96 subjects, 16 males and 80 females, aged 69.2 ± 6.2 years, community-dwelling, and from one of four senior centers in Rhode Island.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Subjects participated in 30 minutes of DE (once per week) and ∼80 minutes of RT (two separate sessions per week) for eight weeks. The DE sessions were led by a registered dietitian.

sec.title: Measurements
sec.p: Anthropometrics (height, weight, waist circumference, hip circumference, and body composition), clinical (blood pressure), biochemical (lipid profile, glucose, and CRP concentrations), and diet quality measured by the Dietary Screening Tool (DST).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A significant change was seen in DST risk categories from baseline to postintervention, χ2 (2)=20.43, p < 0.01. Significant differences were seen in triacylglycerol (p=0.028) as well as in systolic and diastolic blood pressures, weight, waist circumference, hip circumference, percent body fat, fat mass, and body mass index (all p<0.05).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This intervention effectively decreased CHD risk in overweight and obese older adults. Future research is needed to examine the effects of longer DE plus RT interventions with greater weight loss on the lipid profile and CRP concentrations in overweight and obese older adults at risk for CHD.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Coronary heart disease (CHD) is the leading cause of death for men and women over age 65 in the United States (US) 1, 2. Coronary heart disease risk increases with age (3) and over 80% of CHD-related deaths occur in adults ≥ 65 years (4). In 2001, there were approximately 35 million older adults (> 65 years) in the US and researchers expect this population to double by 2030 (5). The growing population of adults > 65 years combined with high CHD death rates, make CHD risk prevention critical 1, 5, 6. Modifiable CHD risk factors include poor dietary intake, physical inactivity, and dyslipidemia and inflammation measures 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14.

On average, older adults have poor dietary habits such as inadequate consumption of whole grains, dark green and orange vegetables, legumes, and milk, and excessive intake of solid fats, added sugars, and sodium (15). Compliance with the Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH) diet improves diet quality and lowers CHD risk by decreasing systolic and diastolic blood pressure (16) and improving lipid and lipoprotein concentrations 1, 7. The DASH diet recommends high consumption of fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy products, whole grains, nuts, poultry, and fish and minimal consumption of total fat, cholesterol, red meat, and sugar-containing beverages 18, 19.

Over 50% of adults ≥ 65 years are considered inactive (20), which increases CHD risk (21). Resistance training (RT) is the preferred intervention to treat sarcopenia (i.e. age-related loss of muscle mass) and its consequences including declines in physical function 22, 23, 24. Additional benefits of RT include improvements in lipid concentrations and blood pressure (25), increases in lean mass (25), and muscle strength 26, 27, and decreases in fat mass 25, 27.

Elevated concentrations of total cholesterol (TC), low density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), triacylglycerol (TAG), and C-Reactive protein (CRP) and low concentrations of high density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) are independent, modifiable CHD risk factors. More than 30% and 40% of males and females > 66 years old, respectively, have dyslipidemia (28). The prevalence of these risk factors increase with age (3) and improving them can decrease CHD incidence and prevalence 1, 2, 9, 10, 11, 13, 17, 29, 30.

A combined behavioral-based diet education (DE) and RT program tailored for at-risk community-dwelling older adults may be an effective strategy for decreasing CHD risk 31, 32, 33, 34. However, few studies have examined this combination on CHD risk in overweight and obese community-dwelling older adults. The purpose of this study was to examine the effects of DE plus RT on dietary quality, blood lipid and CRP concentrations in overweight and obese older adults in a community setting. We hypothesized that participation would significantly improve dietary quality. We also hypothesized that DE plus RT would significantly improve blood lipid and CRP concentrations.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: The study was a community outreach intervention with a quasi-experimental design examining the effect of eight weeks of DE plus RT on CHD risk factors in overweight and obese community-dwelling older adults. The independent variable was time with a single group intervention of DE and RT sessions. Primary dependent variables were dietary quality and concentrations of LDL-C and CRP and secondary dependent variables consisted of the remaining lipids.

sec.title: Subjects
sec.p: Subjects were a convenience sample of older adults attending one of four senior centers in Rhode Island; two urban and two suburban. Subjects were recruited through advertisements and flyers posted in the senior centers’ bulletins, direct mailings, and press releases in local newspapers. Interested subjects completed phone interviews with study staff to determine eligibility. Several orientation sessions were held at each center and potential subjects completed a medical history and read and signed a consent form approved by the Institutional Review Board of the University of Rhode Island.

Inclusion criteria were age 55-80 years, body mass index (BMI) between 25.0-39.9 kg/m2, engage in vigorous exercise ≤ 1 time per week, weight-stable (self-reported within 5%) for past three months, and ability to safely engage in mild to moderate-intensity exercise with muscular exertion. Exclusion criteria were significant or suspected cognitive impairment, severe hearing loss, speech disorder, visual impairment, or any condition that would make prohibit safe participation in an exercise program. Subjects were excluded if they were taking lipid-lowering medications <6 months and other medications for <3 weeks. Subjects were advised to receive medical clearance from their primary care physician prior to beginning the intervention.

A total of 109 subjects completed baseline testing. Thirteen subjects withdrew from the study (5 lost to follow up, 5 due to unrelated personal issues, 2 due to time constraints, and 1 due to a study-related adverse event of a strained muscle). Ninety-six subjects completed post-intervention testing. Although not required, medical clearance was recommended, and 63 (66%) subjects provided written medical clearance from their personal physicians. All described measures below were performed at both baseline and post-intervention.

sec.title: Anthropometrics
sec.p: Height was measured to the nearest 0.25 inch using a portable stadiometer (Detetco, Webb City, MO) and weight was measured to the nearest 0.25 pound using a balance beam scale (Detetco, Webb City, MO). Body mass index was calculated using weight in kilograms/height in meters2. Waist circumference was measured at the top of the upper iliac crest to the nearest 0.25 inch and hip circumference was measured around the broadest circumference of the hips above the gluteal fold to the nearest 0.25 inch using a Gulick tape measure with an attached tensometer (Patterson Medical, Mount Joy, PA). Waist-to-hip ratio was calculated. Body composition was assessed via battery powered handheld bioelectrical impedance analysis (OMRON Fat Loss Monitor model HBF-306C, Omron Health Care Inc, Bannockburn, IL).

sec.title: Clinical
sec.p: Blood pressure was measured with a stethoscope and sphygmomanometer (Diagnostix 752 MLF Mobile Aneroid Sphygmomanometer, American Diagnostic Corporation, Hauppauge, NY) using the subject's left arm after resting in a seated position for five minutes. Two blood pressure measurements were taken one minute apart. If blood pressure measurements exceeded 160/100 mmHg, subjects did not participate in testing or RT.

sec.title: Biochemical
sec.p: Lipid (TC, HDL-C, and TAG), glucose, and CRP concentrations were measured via 12-hour fasted blood samples obtained by fingersticks. Fingersticks were administered by trained study staff and analyzed by the tabletop analyzer, Cholestech LDX (Cholestech Cooperation; Hayward, California). The Friedewald equation was used to determine LDL-C (35).

sec.title: Questionnaires
sec.p: The Dietary Screening Tool (DST) was used to assess dietary intake. The DST is a validated, 24-item questionnaire developed to assess dietary intake and identify nutritional risk among older adults in a community setting. The DST measures overall dietary intake, assesses where a diet may be lacking or in excess of a specific macro- or micro-nutrient, and categorizes individuals into a degree of nutritional risk 36, 37. Based on the DST score (range 0-100 points), subjects are categorized into one of three nutrition risk levels 36, 37; <60, 60-75, and >75 place subjects in at-risk, possible-risk, or not-at-risk, respectively (36). A majority of the DST questions relate to aspects of the DASH diet as DST questions inquire about the consumption of fruits, vegetables, dairy products, whole grains, meats and protein, fats, and sweets.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Study staff visited the senior centers twice a week and delivered 60-minute classes focused on behavioral-based DE and RT. Subjects participated in 30 minutes each of DE and RT on the first visit and ~50 minutes of RT on the second visit for eight weeks. Intervention materials were based off a pilot study (31) and are available upon request.

The DE sessions, led by a registered dietitian, focused on a modified DASH diet for weight loss. Instead of ≤ 27% energy from total fat (16), subjects were encouraged to eat ≤ 35% fat with an emphasis on unsaturated fatty acids since unsaturated fatty acids can provide heart healthy benefits (38). Class topics included an introduction to the DASH diet, self-monitoring, eating healthy during holidays and when eating out, recipe modification, understanding food labels, and maintenance of the DASH diet.

All subjects participated in one ~30 minute session and one ~50 minute session per week of supervised RT on non-consecutive weekdays led by trained exercise physiologists under the supervision of URI faculty and principal investigators. Subjects performed 8-12 repetitions of upper and lower body exercises using light dumbbells, elastic tubing, and ankle weights as resistance. Subjects were instructed to perform the concentric phase of each exercise at ~ 1 second while the eccentric phase was ~ 2-3 seconds. Each subject's individual progress was monitored and resistance was modified throughout the intervention.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Data were analyzed using the Statistical Package for Social Sciences (version 16.0, 2007, SPSS Inc, Chicago, IL). In order to achieve power of 0.8, a minimum of 90 subjects were needed to find a small effect of approximately 0.165 on LDL-C (mg/dL), an effect similar to that found by Obarzanek et al. (17). Approximately 108 subjects were needed for an estimated attrition rate of 15% (39). Normality of data was assessed by skewness and kurtosis values. After removal of four outliers (> 3 standard deviations from the mean) from glucose and log transformations of TAG and LDL-C, normality for these variables was achieved. Back transformed data are presented for ease of interpretation. Continuous variables are shown as mean ± standard deviation and frequencies are shown for categorical variables.

Independent samples t-tests were conducted to compare DST scores, LDL-C concentrations, and CRP concentrations for site types (urban vs. suburban) at baseline to ensure equivalence. To determine if differences existed between completers and non-completers and between subjects taking and not taking lipid-lowering medications in dietary quality, blood lipid concentrations and CRP concentrations, independent samples t-tests were run. To examine change over time in LDL-C and CRP concentrations and to rule out possible interaction with site type, mixed between-within subjects analyses of covariance were used controlling for age and baseline BMI. Since the DST is reported categorically in the literature 36, 37 a chi-square test examined changes in DST risk categories and questions. Secondary analyses consisted of paired samples t-tests to evaluate the impact of the intervention on DST scores as a continuous variable, anthropometric, biochemical, and clinical values. Alpha was set at 0.05.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Subject characteristics are presented in Table 1. The majority of subjects were female (83.3%), white (98.9%), and almost half were taking lipid-lowering medications (49.0%). There were no medication changes during the intervention. Suburban and urban sites accounted for 57.3% and 42.7% of the sample, respectively.Table 1
Baseline Subject Characteristics
Age (years)NMean ± SD
9669.2 ± 6.2
N%
Gender Male1616.7
Female8083.3
Race White9598.9
Hispanic11.0
Education High School or Less2425.0
Some College3435.4
4 Year College Degree (BA/BS)1919.8
Post Bachelor's or Advanced Degree1919.8
Site Suburban5557.3
Urban4142.7
Medications & Supplements* Lipid Lowering4749.0
Anticoagulant1818.8
Anti-inflammatory1717.7
Bone and Health Supplements and/or Medications1616.7
Diabetes99.4
Multivitamin88.3
None1818.8*
Multiple subjects were taking > 1 medication and/or supplement. Subjects taking bone and health supplements were not in addition those taking a multivitamin. Bone and health supplements and/or medications subjects reported using were: calcium, Caltrate, Vitamin D, Vitamin B12,Vitamin C, Folic acid, omega oil, cod liver oil, potassium, magnesium, Fosamax, and Evista.

No differences were seen between site type at baseline for DST scores, LDL-C and CRP concentrations. A significant difference in baseline DST scores was seen between completers and non-completers. Significant differences were seen at baseline and post-intervention between those taking and not taking lipid-lowering medications in TC, LDL-C, and CRP concentrations and DST scores at post-intervention; no significant differences were found for TAG or HDL-C. No significant interaction or main effects for time and site type were found for LDL-C and CRP when controlling for age and baseline BMI. A significant change was seen in DST risk categories from baseline to post-intervention, χ2 (2)=20.43, p<0.01, with 25, 48, and 23 subjects classified at baseline as at-risk, possible-risk, and not-at-risk, respectively, and 9, 47, and 40, respectively, at post-intervention (Figure 1). Though the actual number of subjects at possible-risk did not change, 4 subjects moved up 2 categories, 32 moved up 1 category, 53 remained in the same category, and 7 moved down 1 category (data not shown). Significant changes were seen in fat intake related DST questions, p<0.01 (data not shown).Figure 1
Nutritional Risk at Baseline and Post-Intervention

Results for dietary and biochemical variables are presented in Table 2. When analyzed continuously, the DST score significantly (p<0. 001) increased from baseline to post-intervention. A significant change was seen in TAG (p=0.028) but not in TC, HDL-C, LDL-C, CRP, or glucose.Table 2
Subject's Dietary and Biochemical Values at Baseline and Post-Intervention

DST: Dietary Screening Tool, TC: total cholesterol, HDL-C: high density lipoprotein cholesterol, TAG: triacylglycerol, LDL-C: low density lipoprotein cholesterol, CRP: C-Reactive protein

caption.p: Abnormal CHD Risk Factor Values at Baseline and Post-Intervention

sec.p:
Anthropometric and clinical data are presented in Table 3. Significant decreases were observed in systolic and diastolic blood pressures, weight, waist circumference, hip circumference, percent body fat, fat mass, and BMI (all p<0.05). No significant differences were seen in waist-to-hip ratio and lean mass from baseline to post-intervention.Table 3
Subject's Anthropometric and Clinical Data at Baseline and Post-Intervention

(mmHg)a
unable to obtain subjects measurementb
17 subjects had BF percentages outside the measurable ranges of the machine and 1 subject had a pacemaker and test could not be conducted and as a result fat mass and lean mass could not be calculated on those subjects.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: To our knowledge, no studies have examined the effect of DE plus RT with overweight and obese older adults in a community setting. The primary results of this study indicate that an eight week, community-based, modified DASH DE plus RT intervention is successful at improving CHD risk factors in community-dwelling overweight and obese older adults. First, subjects significantly improved their diet quality measured by the DST. In addition, subjects significantly decreased TAG concentrations. To our knowledge, we are the first, other than the validation studies, to use the DST.

The poor dietary habits of older adults, as defined by the 2005 Healthy Eating Index score, needs improvement (15) and behavioral-based DE has been shown to improve diet quality 33, 40. In the present study, there was a 64% decrease in subjects classified as nutritionally at-risk post-intervention compared to baseline. In addition, there was a 74% increase in those classified as not-at-risk at post-intervention compared to baseline. Previous DASH interventions also reported improved diet quality 16, 17, however few have been conducted in the community with free living community-dwelling older adults at risk for CHD (31). A community-based fruit and vegetable intervention reported a significant increase in the number of subjects consuming ≥ 7 daily servings of fruits and vegetables (33). Another community-based general healthy lifestyle intervention reported a significant increase in percentage of subjects who increased the number of servings of fruit, vegetable, fiber, and calcium rich foods consumed by one or more servings (40). Diets highlighting dietary goals similar to a modified DASH diet are successful at improving the diet quality of community-dwelling older adults.

Triglyceride concentrations <150 mg/dL is recommended (9) because CHD risk increases by 14% and 37% for men and women, respectively, when concentrations are >38.7 mg/dL the optimal level (41). Mean baseline TAG concentration was 157.8 mg/dL and decreased by 12.3 mg/dL to 145.9 mg/dL (p=0.028) at post-intervention. No other lipid and CRP concentrations significantly changed. This study is in contrast to other studies that have observed significant decreases in TC and LDL-C after consuming the DASH diet for 12 weeks (42). Research examining the effect of DASH on CRP concentrations have shown no effect on CRP (42), as reported in this study. However, Villareal et al. reported that a hypocaloric diet and three weekly 90-minute exercise classes focusing on strength, balance, endurance, and flexibility resulted in decreases in TAG (-45 ± 63 mg/dL, p < 0.05) and CRP (-2.5 ± 4.3 mg/L, p < 0.01) in obese older adults (34). It is speculated that additional improvements in LDL-C and CRP values would be evident with a longer intervention period and a more intense and/or more frequent RT program.

Coronary heart disease risk decreased via improved anthropometric and clinical measures in addition to improved dietary quality and TAG concentrations. Improvements in weight status is vital to reducing CHD risk (21). Significant decreases were seen in weight (-1 kilogram), BMI (-0.36 kg/m2), waist circumference (-5.08 centimeters), hip circumference (-5.34 centimeters), percent body fat (-0.4 %), and fat mass (-0.82 kilograms) post-intervention. Abdominal fat is an independent risk factor for disease and examining changes in waist circumference can help provide an estimation of increases or decreases in abdominal fat in the absence of changes in BMI (43). High blood pressure is also a CHD risk factor, and decreases in systolic and diastolic blood pressure (-9.4 and -1.9 mmHg, respectively) were observed. A change of 3 mmHg in systolic blood pressure can result in a 5% decrease in CHD mortality 44, 45. The results of this study are similar to a pilot study that examined the additive effects of RT with DE on CHD risk factors and physical function in 27 overweight and obese males and females (67 ± 4.0 years) randomized into one of two groups: DASH, which received only education on a modified DASH diet, and DASH-RT, which received the same diet information and three 45-minute RT sessions per week (31). The DASH-RT group significantly decreased weight, BMI, percent body fat, diastolic blood pressure, and oxidized LDL-C (31), suggesting the combination of DE plus RT can improve anthropometric, clinical, and biochemical CHD risk factors.

This study is not without limitations. First, there was no control group, as the subjects served as their own controls. This was however, a community-based outreach intervention and was modeled after previous studies 33, 40. Secondly, blood was obtained via fingersticks rather than venous blood draws. However, the Cholestech LDX is valid and reliable (46) and in the community setting the Cholestech LDX is more feasible. Though the DST was not validated in an intervention study, it does measure changes in dietary patterns. In addition, the subjects of the current study (sample size, age, percent females, percent Caucasian, and level of education) are similar to the subjects used in the development of the DST 36, 37. Lastly, although statistically significant weight loss (1 kilogram) was observed, a longer intervention period may have resulted in more clinically significant weight loss and possibly significant improvements in TC, HDL-C, LDL-C, and CRP concentrations.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The results of this study suggest that a community-based DE plus RT intervention effectively decreases CHD risk factors in overweight and obese older adults. Behavioral-based DE on a modified DASH diet and RT for eight weeks significantly improved dietary quality and decreased TAG concentrations in overweight and obese community-dwelling older adults. Future research is needed to examine the effects of longer DE plus RT interventions with greater weight loss on TC, LDL-C, HDL-C, and CRP concentrations in overweight and obese older adults at risk for CHD.
